title: Are most AI trading systems completely useless during earnings, or are discretionary traders just overestimating their edge?
author: Training-Extent9606
selftext:
Every earnings season, the same pattern shows up.

Models struggle.

Discretionary traders get chopped.

And both sides quietly blame execution.

But if you strip it down, the problem is deeper.

AI systems fail because, they’re trained on continuity and earnings are discontinuity

They recognize patterns that simply don’t exist during repricing events.

Discretionary traders fail because they think they understand context but still get trapped in the first move “reading the tape” doesn’t help much when positioning unwinds faster than interpretation.

So here’s the uncomfortable middle ground:

AI doesn’t understand why the market moves

Humans don’t react fast enough when it moves

I’ve been experimenting with a hybrid approach into NVDA:

not using AI for signals, but for stress testing scenarios ahead of time.

Ran a few structured prompts through GetAgent just to map out, what a “good” result actually means vs expectations, how different guidance scenarios could shift positioning, where reactions might fail instead of follow through

It gave me an edge in prediction.

But it did expose how often both models and humans are trading the wrong assumption.

At this point, it feels like neither side really has control during earnings.

The only real edge might be knowing when your framework stops working, and staying out.

Are AI systems fundamentally misaligned for events like this,or are discretionary traders just better at telling themselves a convincing story after the fact?

comments.author: Minimum_Raccoon_1501
comments.body: It seems a lot of ai trading attempts focus on making a profit bot instead of a fundamentals bot
